1.synthesis: King Herod Agrippa kills James the brother of John with the sword (Acts 12:1–2). Seeing it pleases the Jewish leadership, he proceeds to arrest Peter during the Passover feast, intending to bring him out for trial after Passover (Acts 12:3–4). Peter is kept in prison under heavy guard; earnest prayer is made for him by the church (Acts 12:5).
1.historical_context: Herod Agrippa I (grandson of Herod the Great) ruled all of Judea from 41–44 AD, having accumulated his grandfather's full territory through Roman political skill. James son of Zebedee is the first apostle to be martyred in Acts—his death receives only one verse, in stark contrast to Stephen's extended martyrdom narrative. The timing (Passover, the same season as Jesus' execution) is significant: Herod is repeating the pattern of Roman-Jewish collaboration against the church's leadership. The church's response—prayer (Acts 12:5)—is its sole resource, and it becomes the mechanism of Peter's deliverance.
1.literary_structure: The placement of James's execution alongside Peter's imprisonment creates a deliberate juxtaposition: two apostles face the same threat; one is killed, one is delivered. Luke does not explain the difference—he presents it without theodicy. The emphasis falls on the praying community rather than on the martyrdom or the imprisonment. Prayer is the church's counter-move to imperial power, and the narrative will demonstrate its efficacy. The 'earnest prayer' (proseuchē ektenos) of v.5 uses the same Greek word group as Jesus' Gethsemane prayer (Luke 22:44).
1.theological_application: The juxtaposition of James's martyrdom and Peter's imprisonment without explanation confronts the church with the mystery of divine providence: the same sovereign God who delivers Peter does not prevent James's death. The church cannot construct a theology of divine protection that promises immunity from suffering and death—James and Stephen have demonstrated otherwise. What the church does have is the resource of earnest, communal prayer, which the narrative validates as genuinely effective. The mystery is not that prayer works only sometimes but that 'working' can mean both miraculous deliverance and faithful death. The church's 'earnest prayer' for Peter is continuous, unceasing intercession—the same quality Jesus demonstrated in Gethsemane. Luke does not invite the reader to calculate why James died and Peter lived; he models instead the community's response to both outcomes: faithful intercession directed to the God who holds all outcomes, whose purposes are accomplished through martyrdom and miraculous deliverance alike. The one posture the church must not adopt in the face of state-sponsored violence against its leaders is resignation to silence.
1.christology: James's martyrdom at Herod's sword completes the trajectory that began with Stephen's stoning: the apostolic community follows its Lord into the pattern of rejection and death. James and John had asked to sit at Jesus' right and left hands in his glory; Jesus told them they would drink his cup (Mark 10:38–39). James's death fulfills that prediction—he has drunk the cup. Peter's imprisonment during Passover is another Passover crisis requiring a new exodus—the theme that has run through Acts as the risen Christ delivers his people from every form of bondage.

6.synthesis: The night before his trial, Peter is sleeping between two soldiers, bound with chains, with guards at the door—and an angel wakes him: 'Get up quickly.' The chains fall from his hands (Acts 12:6–7). He dresses, follows the angel, passes through iron gates that open of their own accord, and reaches the street before the angel departs (Acts 12:8–10). He goes to the house of Mary the mother of John Mark, where many are gathered praying. A servant girl named Rhoda hears his voice but in her joy forgets to open the gate and reports to the others, who declare she is out of her mind (Acts 12:12–15). Peter continues knocking; they open and are astonished. He tells them to report to James and the brothers, then departs (Acts 12:16–17).
6.historical_context: The escape narrative is rich in Exodus typology: the night-time deliverance, the chains falling off, the passing through gates, the instruction to dress and follow—all echo the Passover night. Peter's chains may be an ironic symbol: the same Passover festival that killed Jesus and imprisoned Peter is the context of his deliverance. The house of Mary the mother of John Mark is presumably a large house used as a meeting place—John Mark will become significant in Acts 13 and the source of Gospel of Mark. The Jerusalem 'James' to whom Peter reports is James the Lord's brother (Galatians 1:19), who leads the Jerusalem church after the apostles scatter.
6.literary_structure: The escape narrative is structured with dark comedy: the praying community is so astonished when their prayer is answered that they insist Rhoda must be wrong, while Peter stands at the gate knocking. The community's astonishment despite their prayer models the gap between theoretical faith ('we are praying for his release') and operative faith ('his release is actually happening'). The passage demonstrates Luke's narrative skill: the suspense of the escape, the comedy of the gate, the wonder of the reunion, and the purposeful departure before dawn all move with cinematic pacing.
6.theological_application: The gap between the praying community's stated prayer ('earnest prayer') and their astonished disbelief when it is answered is not a condemnation but an accurate portrait of human faith: we pray with genuine need, but our imagination of what God will actually do is limited. The prayer was real; God's answer exceeded what they imagined possible. The church does not need perfect faith for God to act—it needs persistent, earnest prayer directed to the God who can do more than we ask or imagine (Ephesians 3:20). Rhoda's joy, the community's skepticism, and their final astonishment are the authentic emotional range of a community learning to expect the miraculous.
6.christology: Peter's angelic deliverance from prison is the second new-exodus liberation in Acts (following the apostles' release in chapter 5). Each liberation demonstrates that the risen Christ's authority exceeds the state's capacity to contain his witnesses. The chains that fall from Peter's hands echo the spiritual liberation that the risen Christ proclaimed (Luke 4:18)—his physical bondage broken by angelic action is an enacted parable of the deeper liberation he brings. Peter's instruction to report to James (v.17) marks the transition of Jerusalem church leadership and Peter's own movement toward wider mission.

19.synthesis: In the morning, the soldiers are in consternation; Herod has the guards examined and executed (Acts 12:18–19). Then Herod goes to Caesarea. The people of Tyre and Sidon sue for peace and arrange an audience; on an appointed day Herod puts on his royal robes, delivers a speech, and the people shout: 'The voice of a god, and not of a man!' Immediately an angel of the Lord strikes him because he did not give God the glory; he is eaten by worms and dies (Acts 12:20–23). But the word of God increases and multiplies (Acts 12:24). Barnabas and Saul return to Antioch (Acts 12:25).
19.historical_context: Josephus provides a detailed account of Herod Agrippa's death at Caesarea in Antiquities 19.8.2: during games in honor of Claudius, he appeared in a silver robe that gleamed in the sun; owls appeared (a bad omen); he was immediately struck with pain and died five days later. Josephus attributes the death to divine punishment without naming God specifically. Luke's account emphasizes the angelic agency and the reason ('he did not give God the glory')—the same sin of claiming divine honor that was refused by Peter in Cornelius's home. The juxtaposition of Herod's death and the word's growth (vv.23–24) is Luke's theological point: the power that opposes the word is destroyed; the word multiplies.
19.literary_structure: The Herod episode provides the pattern for every human authority that claims divine status in Acts: it is judged by the God who alone receives glory. The growth summary of v.24 is the sixth of Luke's progress statements and follows the pattern of every persecution episode in Acts: opposition → community response (prayer) → divine deliverance → continued growth. The return of Barnabas and Saul (v.25) bridges to chapter 13's missionary journey, closing the Jerusalem narrative and opening the mission narrative.
19.theological_application: Herod's death is the NT's clearest example of divine judgment on the sin of accepting divine honor. The people's acclamation ('a god, not a man!') and Herod's failure to correct them is the precise inversion of Peter's correction of Cornelius ('stand up, I too am a man'). The church's ongoing witness in the world must refuse the temptation to claim more than creaturely authority—even when crowds offer it. The growth of the word that immediately follows Herod's death (v.24) establishes that no human authority, however powerful, can permanently contain the gospel: the word outlasts its opponents.
19.christology: The contrast between Herod's death (for claiming divine glory) and the risen Christ's exaltation (given all authority by the Father) is the Acts narrative's implicit argument: only one figure has rightfully received what Herod tried to seize—the one who humbled himself and was exalted (Philippians 2:9–11). The growth of 'the word of God' (v.24) is the growth of the proclamation of Jesus as Lord—the one whose authority no Herod can overthrow, whose word no chains can bind, whose community no sword can finally silence.
